edges.description: Anomalous aortic origin with interarterial or intramural course sets the anatomic stage for dynamic, exercise-triggered compression and kinking of the proximal coronary segment.
Anomalous origin of the left coronary from the pulmonary trunk establishes the low-pressure perfusion bed that, once postnatal pulmonary vascular resistance falls, drives coronary steal.
edges.target: ALCAPA coronary steal and low-pressure perfusion

edges.description: Failed coronary ostial formation or septation of the outflow tract produces fixed structural obstruction to antegrade coronary flow from the aortic root.
Exercise-induced reduction in coronary luminal area drives myocardial ischemia and ventricular arrhythmia; SCD risk concentrates in young athletes during or just after vigorous exertion.
Sustained low-pressure perfusion and coronary steal produce chronic subendocardial ischemia, infantile dilated cardiomyopathy, mitral regurgitation, and heart failure.
edges.source: ALCAPA coronary steal and low-pressure perfusion

edges.description: Fixed obstruction to coronary inflow drives neonatal ischemia and infantile cardiomyopathy, often presenting as early heart failure or sudden death.

nodes.description: Subtype-specific mechanism for AAOCA. The anomalous proximal segment may be compressed between the aorta and pulmonary trunk (interarterial course), tunnel through the aortic wall as an intramural segment with a slit-like ostium, kink at an acute take-off angle, or stretch on exertion when aortopulmonary distention increases. These mechanisms reduce coronary luminal area and flow reserve during exercise, producing dynamic ischemia that can trigger lethal ventricular arrhythmias.

cell_types: smooth muscle cell of the coronary artery

evidence.reference_title: Anomalous Aortic Origin of a Coronary Artery in Pediatric Patients.
evidence.snippet: Anomalous left coronary artery from the right aortic sinus (L-AAOCA) with interarterial course carries a high risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD); therefore, current guidelines recommend exercise restriction and surgical intervention.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Coronary Artery Anomalies and Sudden Cardiac Death.
evidence.snippet: Although rare, congenital CAAs confer a high risk of myocardial ischemia and SCD, especially in young, previously "healthy" athletes during or immediately after vigorous exertion.

nodes.description: Subtype-specific mechanism for ALCAPA. The postnatal fall in pulmonary vascular resistance leaves the left coronary territory perfused at low pressure from the pulmonary trunk. Once intercoronary collaterals develop, blood is shunted retrograde from the right coronary into the pulmonary trunk (coronary steal), producing chronic subendocardial ischemia of the left ventricle and the classic infantile dilated cardiomyopathy with mitral regurgitation.
nodes.id: ALCAPA coronary steal and low-pressure perfusion

evidence.reference_title: Mid-Term Outcome for Anomalous Origin of the Left Coronary Artery From the Pulmonary Artery.
evidence.snippet: Overall, 114 (82.6%) patients received coronary reimplantation, 21 (15.2%) underwent Takeuchi repair, and three (2.1%) were treated by closure of the ALCAPA plus coronary artery bypass graft.

nodes.description: Coronary arteries form during embryogenesis through coordinated contributions of the proepicardium, epicardial epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), endothelial progenitor migration into the developing myocardium, and connection of the nascent coronary plexus to the aortic root at the sinuses of Valsalva. Aberrations in coronary vasculogenesis, coronary stem formation, or septation of the outflow tract can produce anomalous origin from the aorta or pulmonary trunk, ostial atresia or stenosis, single coronary artery, or fistulous persistence of intramural sinusoidal connections. Cardiac neural crest cells and second heart field derivatives shape the outflow-tract anatomy that determines the final position of coronary ostia.

cell_types: smooth muscle cell of the coronary artery

evidence.snippet: Coronary artery anomalies (CAAs) are a group of congenital conditions characterized by abnormal origin or course of any of the 3 main epicardial coronary arteries.

nodes.description: Subtype-specific mechanism for congenital coronary atresia/ostial stenosis. There is fixed structural obstruction to antegrade coronary flow from the aortic root. Unlike AAOCA (dynamic interference) and ALCAPA (perfusion mismatch), obstruction is anatomic and constant, producing neonatal/infantile ischemia, cardiomyopathy, and often early death.

cell_types: smooth muscle cell of the coronary artery

evidence.reference_title: Early neonatal death and congenital left coronary abnormalities: ostial atresia, stenosis and anomalous aortic origin.
evidence.snippet: Congenital coronary ostial abnormalities can lead to severe heart failure and unexpected neonatal death.

nodes.description: Demand-induced myocardial ischemia produced by AAOCA mechanical interference, ALCAPA coronary steal, or fixed ostial stenosis creates regional electrophysiological heterogeneity, fibrosis, and ventricular arrhythmias that can culminate in sudden cardiac death. In infants with ALCAPA and severe ostial obstruction, sustained ischemia leads to left ventricular dysfunction, mitral regurgitation, and dilated cardiomyopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Coronary Artery Anomalies and Sudden Cardiac Death.
evidence.snippet: Coronary artery anomalies (CAAs) are a heterogeneous group of rare congenital diseases whose features and pathophysiological mechanisms are extremely variable, ranging from silent anomalies to sudden cardiac death (SCD) in the most severe cases.

evidence.reference_title: Mid-Term Outcome for Anomalous Origin of the Left Coronary Artery From the Pulmonary Artery.
evidence.snippet: In patients with decreased preoperative left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) (<50%), the LVEF significantly improved from 25% to 33% at discharge (p = 0.001).
